elements.sent-2411-001: Functional data analysis has gained significant attention due to its wide applicability.
elements.sent-2411-002: This research explores the extension of statistical analysis methods for functional data, with a primary focus on supervised classification techniques.
elements.sent-2411-003: It provides a review on the existing depth-based methods used in functional data samples.
elements.sent-2411-004: Building on this foundation, it introduces an extremality-based approach, which takes the modified epigraph and hypograph indexes properties as classification techniques.
elements.sent-2411-005: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the classifier, it is applied to both real-world and synthetic data sets.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results show its efficacy in accurately classifying functional data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, the classifier is used to analyze the fluctuations in the S\&P 500 stock value.
elements.sent-2411-008: This research contributes to the field of functional data analysis by introducing a new extremality-based classifier.
elements.sent-2411-009: The successful application to various data sets shows its potential for supervised classification tasks and provides valuable insights into financial data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-010: Data processing and analysis has become essential for understanding various phenomena, whether found in research, business, politics, or other domains, it provides interesting insights and guide to decision making.
elements.sent-2411-011: As we adapt to an evolving world based on data, our approach to data analysis must evolve as well.
elements.sent-2411-012: This has led to the emergence of functional data analysis, which enables us to model data from an innovative perspective, in this particular case, as a function.
elements.sent-2411-013: The concept of functional data, first coned by \cite{bib1 has been explored over the years, providing interesting research and findings.}
elements.sent-2411-014: As this concept develops, the demand for statistical techniques customized to its distinct characteristics also grows.
elements.sent-2411-015: Traditional statistical techniques are extended to analyze this new data form.
elements.sent-2411-016: Approaches like mean and covariance computations have been expanded to serve as descriptive tools for functional data \cite{bib2 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Additionally, more advanced techniques such as classification \cite{bib3 , clustering \cite{bib4 , principal component analysis \cite{bib5 , homogeinity tests \cite{bib6 and so on, have been adapted to suit functional data analysis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This article is centered around the classification aspect of functional data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-019: Major advances on classification have been made using functional depth \cite{bib7 , the most successful classifiers, the DD-classifier \cite{bib8 and the $DD^G$-classifier \cite{bib9 , provide a great example of how functional depth is applied successfully for classification.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: For this research, we plan on extending these classification methods to incorporate function extremality indexes.
elements.sent-2411-021: Extremality for functional data \cite{bib10 provides a natural ordering for sample curves by introducing the definitions of the hypograph and epigraph indexes.}
elements.sent-2411-022: These concepts have been used to develop different methodologies in the functional data context, such as the outliergram \cite{bib11 , the functional boxplot \cite{bib12 , rank tests \cite{bib13 and clustering \cite{bib14 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Giving such promising results, they inspire to explore different methodologies, such as classification.
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, the purpose of this research is to explore how the methodology of existing depth-based classifiers can be extended to extremality-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-025: It shows the extension of the methodology to extremality concepts and introduces the EE-plot and EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-026: Alongside, it provides illustration of their functionality by creating experiments using synthetic and real data provided by R libraries.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, a real application of the classifier will used to predict the S\&P stock value fluctuation.
elements.sent-2411-028: The subsequent sections of this article are structured as follows: Section \ref{sec2 introduces fundamental preliminary concepts related to the depth-based classifiers and function extremality: epigraph and hypograph.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section \ref{sec3 , the EE-plot and EE-classifier are described.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Section \ref{sec4 presents results obtained using the EE-classifier with simulated and real datasets, followed by an application to S\&P stock value changes in Section \ref{sec5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, Sections \ref{sec6 offer concluding remarks followed by acknowledgement and references.}
elements.sent-2411-032: It remains helpful to take a closer look at some essential concepts that form the basis of our research.
elements.sent-2411-033: The proposed method for classifying functional data is built upon the principles of the DD-classifier \cite{bib8 and the $DD^G$-classifier \cite{bib9 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: To gain a better grasp of our approach, these concepts will be defined.
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, understanding the concepts of function epigraph and hypograph is essential to fully comprehend the new ideas introduced in this article.
elements.sent-2411-036: The DD-plot \cite{bib20 illustrates the depth values of the sample in relation to two distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-037: This transition takes the data from the functional space to the depth-depth space.
elements.sent-2411-038: For the DD-plot you could use any of the implemented depths, some of the most common functional depths are reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-039: Fraiman and Muniz (FM) depth: Proposed by \cite{bib21 , provides the depth measurements for the $i-th$ data point.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Given a sample $x_1,...,x_N$ of functions defined on the interval $[0,T]$ and $S_t$ = $(x_1(t), ...
elements.sent-2411-041: Let the empirical distribution of the sample $S_t$ denoted by $F_{N,t}$ and let $Z_i(t)$ a univariate depth of $x_i(t)$ in this sample.
elements.sent-2411-042: The FM depth for the $i-th$ data point is given by the following integral: \begin{equation}FM_i = \int_{0}^{T} Z_i(t)dt \label{eq:0.0}\end{equation} For the original definition $Z_i(t)$ = $1 - |1/2 - F_{N,t (x_i(t))|$, but this can change according the different univariate depths to consider, which could be the Tukey depth, the Simplicial depth, Likelihood depth and the Mahalanobis depth.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The population h-mode depth of a datum $x_0$ is given by: \begin{equation}f_h(x_0) = E[K(m(x_0, X)/h)] \label{eq:0.1}\end{equation} where $X$ is the random element describing the population, $m$ is the suitable metric, $K(t)$ is an asymmetric kernel and $h$ is the bandwidth parameter.
elements.sent-2411-044: Given a random sample $x_1, ..., x_N$ of X, the empirical h-mode depth is defined as: \begin{equation}\hat{f}_h(x_0) = N^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^N K(m(x_0, x_i)/h) \label{eq:0.2}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: Random projections (RP) depth: This method proposed by \cite{bib22 uses the univariate half space depth and summarizes the depths of the projection through the mean.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The definition for the depth is then given by: \begin{equation}RP(x) = R^-1 \sum_{r=1}^RD_{a_r}(x) \label{eq:0.3}\end{equation} where $D{a_r (x)$ is the depth associated with the $r-th$ projection and using $R=50$ as the default choice.}
elements.sent-2411-047: If we wanted to extend this method to the multivariate case we could also construct a weighted depth from the depth of each component.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{10_dd_plot_1.eps} \caption{Similar distributions} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{10_dd_plot_2.eps} \caption{Different distributions} \end{subfigure} \caption{DD-plot illustration with different $X$ and $Y$ distributions} \label{fig:ddplot}
elements.sent-2411-048: The formal definition of the DD-plot is given by \cite{bib20 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Let us consider two distinct distributions, $F$ and $G$ defined on $\mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Given an invariant depth measure denoted as $D(\cdot)$, $DD(F,G)$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}DD(F,G) = \{(D_{F}(x),D_{G}(x)), \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^d \} \label{eq:1}\end{equation} When the underlying distribution $F$ is unknown, with a given dataset $X_{1 , ...}
elements.sent-2411-051: This function produces samples from a specified multivariate normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-052: Figure \ref{fig:ddplot shows the DD-plot using the Mahalanobis depth, \ref{fig:ddplot (a) with two $ N (0,0) $ distributions and Figure \ref{fig:ddplot (b) with one $ N (0,0) $ distribution and another $ N (0,1) $ distribution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The research presented in \cite{bib20 provides valuable insights into the characterization of different curves using the DD-plot.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In instances where the two distributions are similar, the DD-plot should be concentrated along the diagonal line, as exemplified in Figure \ref{fig:ddplot (a).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Notable patterns or deviations from this line, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:ddplot (b), indicate specific differences between distributions F and G.}
elements.sent-2411-056: These distinctions may arise from a location shift, manifesting as a heart-shaped DD-plot; a scale difference, resulting in an arched DD-plot above the diagonal line; skewness differences, producing an asymmetric arch on the DD-plot; or kurtosis differences, leading to a shift in the lower part of the DD-plot to one side of the diagonal line.
elements.sent-2411-057: This visual tool offers a comprehensive understanding of the diverse characteristics that can be characterized through the DD-plot.
elements.sent-2411-058: This characterization from the DD-plot creates a space where one could use different classification methods to specify whether the two distributions have similar characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-059: That is where the DD-classifier and the $DD^G$-classifier use their methodologies to determine the similarities between distributions.
elements.sent-2411-060: The distinction between these two classifiers remains as the way of how both classifiers create classification rules based on the DD-plot.
elements.sent-2411-061: The DD-classifier transforms the data from the functional space to the depth-depth space and aims to find the optimal function that separates the groups based on the minimal classification error.
elements.sent-2411-062: Conversely, the $DD^G$-classifier provides classification based on traditional classification methods such as Linear Discriminant Analysis, Quadratic Discriminant Analysis, k-Nearest Neighbors, and others.
elements.sent-2411-063: Besides, unlike the $DD$-classifier, which allows only two classification groups at a time, the $DD^G$-Classifier extends this capability to more than two groups.
elements.sent-2411-064: The concepts of extremality include the epigraph and hypograph of a function.
elements.sent-2411-065: The hypograph of a function shows the points situated either directly on or below its graph.
elements.sent-2411-066: On the contrary, the epigraph shows points either directly on or above the graph of the function.
elements.sent-2411-067: In a formal sense, based on \cite{bib15 , let $C(\mathcal{I )$ denote the space of continuous functions defined within a compact interval $\mathcal{I}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Let us consider a stochastic process $X$ with sample paths in $C(\mathcal{I )$ and distributed according to $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We will denote the graph of the function $x$ in $C(\mathcal{I )$ as $G(x)$, thus $G(x) = {(t,x(t)), t \in \mathcal{I }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Following this, the definitions for the hypograph and the epigraph of a function $x$ in $C(\mathcal{I )$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \begin{split} hyp(x) = {(t,y) \in \mathcal{I} \times \mathbb{R} : y > x(t) }\ epi(x) = {(t,y) \in \mathcal{I} \times \mathbb{R} : y \leq x(t)} \ \end{split} \end{cases} \label{eq:4}\end{equation} The hypograph and epigraph indexes were introduced \cite{bib10 to provide an order for the curves in functional data.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}  hyp(x) = {(t,y) \in I \times R : y > x(t) }\ epi(x) = {(t,y) \in I \times R : y \leq x(t)} \   \label{eq:4}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: These indexes provide a proportion of functions within the sample whose graphs fall within the hypograph or epigraph of function $x$.
elements.sent-2411-073: This quantification is obtained by subtracting the proportion of curves in the sample that lie below or above $x$ from one.
elements.sent-2411-074: Formally, these can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \begin{split} HI(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( G(x_i) \subset hyp(x) ) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( x_i(t) \leq x(t), t \in \mathcal{I}) EI(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( G(x_i) \subset epi(x) ) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( x_i(t) \geq x(t), t \in \mathcal{I}) \end{split} \end{cases} \label{eq:5}\end{equation} where $I(A)$ is the indicator function of the set A.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}  HI(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( G(x_i) \subset hyp(x) ) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( x_i(t) \leq x(t), t \in I) EI(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( G(x_i) \subset epi(x) ) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n I( x_i(t) \geq x(t), t \in I)   \label{eq:5}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: The concepts of hypograph and epigraph indexes may encounter limitations in scenarios where curve classifications involve closely or widely spaced curves \cite{bib11 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Consequently, modified versions of these indices were also introduced \cite{bib15 to address such situations: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \begin{split} MHI(x) = \frac{1}{n \lambda(\mathcal{I})} \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda \{t \in \mathcal{I}: x_i(t) \leq x(t) \} MEI(x) = \frac{1}{n \lambda(\mathcal{I})} \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda \{t \in \mathcal{I}: x_i(t) \geq x(t) \} \end{split} \end{cases} \label{eq:6}\end{equation} where $\lambda$ stands for the Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}  MHI(x) = \frac{1}{n \lambda(I)} \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda \{t \in I: x_i(t) \leq x(t) \} MEI(x) = \frac{1}{n \lambda(I)} \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda \{t \in I: x_i(t) \geq x(t) \}   \label{eq:6}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-077: One important characteristic given by MHI and MEI, described in \cite{bib14 , is that they comply the following relation $MHI(x) - MEI(x)= \frac{1 {n}$, concluding that they are linearly dependent.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: As described in Section \ref{sec2 , the modified epigraph and hypograph indexes, show characteristics for curve samples, giving them an order, even when the curves are closely or widely spaced.}
elements.sent-2411-079: This provides information about distribution's similarities if one were to compare them.
elements.sent-2411-080: Visualization tools to provide comparison between curve samples have been used before, such as in \cite{bib13 where they provide homogeneity testing between samples and in \cite{bib14 where clustering techniques are applied to separate them.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Therefore, we aim to explore a visualization tool which we name as Extremality-Extremality (EE) plot as a method to apply classification techniques and introduce a new classification method named as the Extremality-Extremality (EE) classifier.
elements.sent-2411-082: The EE-plot illustrates the modified epigraph or hypograph indexes of the sample combined by two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-083: Following a similar methodology as in equation \ref{eq:1 , consider $F$ and $G$ two different distributions in $\mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $MEI(\cdot)$ the modified epigraph index and $MHI(\cdot)$ the modified hypograph index, then EE(F,G) can be defined in two ways $EE_{H (F,G)$ for when the modified hypograph index is used and $EE_{E (F,G)$ for when the modified epigraph index is used, the definitions are given as: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} EE_{H}(F,G) = \{(MHI_{F}(x), MHI_{G}(x)), \ \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^d \} EE_{E}(F,G) = \{(MEI_{F}(x), MEI_{G}(x)), \ \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^d \} \end{cases} \label{eq:6}\end{equation} The EE-plot establishes a new space derived from the characteristics of functional data, therefore providing a transformation from the functional space into $\mathbb{R ^2$ for our data.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation} EE_{H}(F,G) = \{(MHI_{F}(x), MHI_{G}(x)), \ \forall x \in R^d \} EE_{E}(F,G) = \{(MEI_{F}(x), MEI_{G}(x)), \ \forall x \in R^d \}  \label{eq:6}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let us suppose we have two different population distributions for the samples $\mathcal{X = \{X_1, .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Notably, Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments (b), \ref{fig:1_experiments (c), \ref{fig:1_experiments (e), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (f) depict how the $EE_{H}$ and $EE_{E}$ exhibit a mirrored relationship, highlighting the underlying behavior given by the relation $MHI(x) - MEI(x)= \frac{1 {n}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: In order to provide characterization for the EE-plots we observe the results derived from Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments and \ref{fig:2_experiments .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: In cases of similar distributions, a distinct 45-degree cluster line is evident.
elements.sent-2411-088: For sinusoidal distributions, a notable chromosome-like pattern emerges.
elements.sent-2411-089: As the amplitude widens or dispersion increases within the same chromosome shape, the data points become more scattered.
elements.sent-2411-090: Notably, distributions that do not overlap exhibit an L shape in the corners of the EE-plot.
elements.sent-2411-091: With slight overlapping, this L shape transforms into a more rounded form at the corners.
elements.sent-2411-092: Therefore, as mentioned when two sample data follow the same distribution, data points cluster around the 45-degree line on the plot.
elements.sent-2411-093: This pattern is evident in Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments (a), \ref{fig:1_experiments (b), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (c).}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, as the distributions begin to diverge, points within the EE-plot start to disperse along the axis, leading to interesting visual patterns as demonstrated in \ref{fig:1_experiments (d), \ref{fig:1_experiments (e), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (f).}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In instances where the curves do not overlap, a similar pattern emerges: data points within the EE-plot remain distinct, akin to what is depicted in Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments (g), \ref{fig:1_experiments (h), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (i).}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: In order to provide the characterization of EE-plots, we analyze the outcomes depicted in Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments and \ref{fig:2_experiments .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: When distributions are similar, a clear 45-degree cluster line becomes evident, as observed in Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments (a), \ref{fig:1_experiments (b), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (c).}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: For sinusoidal overlapping distributions, presented in Experiments 2, 4 and 5 a distinctive chromosome-like pattern emerges, and as the amplitude or dispersion increases within this pattern, data points disperse, creating a scattered effect.
elements.sent-2411-099: The visual patterns in the EE-plots show transformations as distributions vary.
elements.sent-2411-100: In instances where curves do not overlap, a discernible L shape appears in the corners of the EE-plot, as illustrated in Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments (g), \ref{fig:1_experiments (h), and \ref{fig:1_experiments (i).}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: With slight overlapping, this L shape transitions into a more rounded form at the corners, introducing interesting visual nuances.
elements.sent-2411-102: These patterns offer valuable insights into the behavior of EE-plots, providing a visual means to interpret similarities and differences between distributions.
elements.figure-2411-002: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm, keepaspectratio=true]{2_sen_sen_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 1: Distribution behavior} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{2_sen_sen_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 1: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{2_sen_sen_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 1: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm, keepaspectratio=true]{1_sen_cos_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 2: Distribution behavior} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{1_sen_cos_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 2: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{1_sen_cos_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 2: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm, keepaspectratio=true]{3_sen_sen_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 3: Distribution behavior} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{3_sen_sen_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 3: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm, keepaspectratio=true]{3_sen_sen_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 3: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Illustration of EE-plots given by two distributions from different experiments} \label{fig:1_experiments}
elements.sent-2411-103: The $\mathbb{R ^2$ space given by the EE-plot, provides patterns of how the groups are distributed.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, as in \cite{bib9 , the groups can be separated by using traditional classification methods.}
elements.sent-2411-105: A set of methods are proposed based on different techniques such as based on discriminant analysis (LDA, QDA), machine learning methods (SVM, Random Forest) and Nonparametric classification methods (kNN).
elements.sent-2411-106: One could implement different classification methods as there is no restriction on the data anymore to grasp their performance.
elements.sent-2411-107: The EE-classifier works by taking two groups of data given by different distributions, measuring the modified epigraph or hypograph index values per curve for each distribution, creating the EE-plot and lastly inside the $\mathbb{R ^2$ space classify according the proposed classification methodologies.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The results given by the different methods are measured in terms of accuracy as a metric.
elements.sent-2411-109: Note that the classifier works with both modified epigraph or hypograph indexes, as the EE-plot using one provides a mirrored version of the other.
elements.sent-2411-110: In this article, both indexes will be shown in order to demonstrate how the method is still useful when both of them are used.
elements.figure-2411-003: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{4_amp_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 4: Distribution behaviour} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{4_amp_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 4: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{4_amp_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 4: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{5_rui_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 5: Distribution behaviour} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{5_rui_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 5: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{5_rui_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 5: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{6_func_sim.eps} \caption{Experiment 6: Distribution behaviour} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{6_func_epi_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 6: $EE_{E}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{6_func_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{Experiment 6: $EE_{H}$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Illustration of EE-plots given by two distributions for different experiments} \label{fig:2_experiments}
elements.sent-2411-111: To evaluate the efficacy of the new EE-classifier we intend to assess the method using both synthetic data and real-world data sourced from R libraries.
elements.sent-2411-112: By comparing results and accuracy metrics for the classification, we aim to present an understanding of its capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-113: Moreover, we will include cross-validation accuracy metrics to demonstrate how the method performs across various data resampling, thereby providing confidence intervals for the metrics and outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the synthetic data demonstration we use the data distributions given in both Figures \ref{fig:1_experiments and \ref{fig:2_experiments .}}
elements.sent-2411-115: The synthetic data was created with the generate\_gauss\_fdata function from the roahd package.
elements.sent-2411-116: This function generates a dataset of univariate functional data with a desired mean and covariance function.
elements.sent-2411-117: It takes as input the centerline of the distribution and a covariance operator.
elements.sent-2411-118: The centerline of the distribution is given by a set of functions and the covariance operator is created by the function exp\_cov\_function, which takes in two different outputs $\alpha$ and $\beta$, they respectively correspond to the amplitude and dispersion of the parameters inside the exponential covariance function.
elements.sent-2411-119: Using different variations of this functions, six experiments were conducted in order to grasp the functionality of the EE-classifier, these are described in Table \ref{tab:experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-120: The distributions generated for the experiments consist on 200 samples per group with 100 data points each.
elements.sent-2411-121: The construction of these experiments was based on how the EE-classifier would perform when using functional data with different characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-122: By using the same distribution (Experiment 1), different distribution with complementary functions, such as $sine$ ans $cosine$ (Experiment 2), non overlapping distributions (Experiment 3), distributions with wider amplitude (Experiment 4), distributions with higher dispersion (Experiment 5) and distributions with different functions as centerlines (Experiment 6).
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Experiment and parameters definitions used for the illustration of the EE-classifier}\label{tab:experiments} \tabcolsep=15.5pt \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} Experiment Centerline $\alpha$ $\beta$ \multirow{2}*{1} $sin(\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 $sin(\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 \multirow{2}*{2} $sin(\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 $cos(\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 \multirow{2}*{3} $sin(\pi)-2$ 0.2 0.3 $sin(\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 \multirow{2}*{4} $sin(2\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 $cos(2\pi)$ 0.7 0.3 \multirow{2}*{5} $sin(2\pi)$ 0.2 0.3 $cos(2\pi)$ 0.2 0.9 \multirow{2}*{6} $x^2 - 1$ 0.2 0.3 $x^2$ 0.2 0.9 \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{1_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{2_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{3_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 3} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{4_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 4} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{5_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 5} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=25cm,keepaspectratio=true]{6_results.eps} \caption{Experiment 6} \end{subfigure} \caption{Accuracy metrics given by each experiment and classifier using 100- Fold cross-validation as train control} \label{fig:results_synthetics}
elements.sent-2411-123: The EE-classifier was applied to these experiments, by using both the MEI and MHI for the EE-plot, and using the different classifiers (LDA, QDA, kNN, SVM and RF).
elements.sent-2411-124: To test the results we divide the sample into training (80\%) and testing (20\%) and train the methods using 100-Fold cross-validation as train control.
elements.sent-2411-125: To establish a benchmark, alongside the EE-classifier, we implemented the $DD^G$-classifier with the Fraiman \& Muniz depth in conjunction with several classifiers (LDA, QDA, and kNN).
elements.sent-2411-126: The selection of classifiers mirrors those chosen for the EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-127: This comparative analysis seeks to offer insights into the performance of the EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-128: The results for each experiment are illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:results_synthetics .}
elements.sent-2411-129: In the first experiment, where the data is generated from the same distribution, all classifiers yield accuracy values around $\sim50\%$.
elements.sent-2411-130: This observation highlights their susceptibility to confusion when classifying closely positioned curves.
elements.sent-2411-131: Notably, the $DD^G$-classifier exhibits slightly superior performance in this scenario, but the EE-classifier emerges as a competitive alternative, offering smaller confidence intervals for accuracy metrics.
elements.sent-2411-132: The LDA classifier, whether utilizing the $DD^G$-classifier or the EE-classifier, excels primarily when curves do not overlap, as demonstrated in the third experiment.
elements.sent-2411-133: This aligns with the characteristics of the LDA classifier, which relies on linear functions to segregate groups.
elements.sent-2411-134: Conversely, other classifiers (QDA, kNN, SVM, and RF) consistently achieve high classification accuracy values for both $EE_E$ and $EE_H$, with QDA generally providing the best overall results.
elements.sent-2411-135: In the fourth and fifth experiments, where wider amplitude and higher dispersion are introduced, the classifiers' performance remains relatively consistent.
elements.sent-2411-136: This suggests that these variations do not significantly impact classifier efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-137: Interestingly, the EE-classifier demonstrates comparable results to the $DD^G$-classifier in these experiments.
elements.sent-2411-138: The evaluation of the EE-classifier on real data was performed with three different functional data sets which can all be found inside the R package fda.usc.
elements.sent-2411-139: The first data set, Berkeley Growth Study shows heights of 39 boys and 54 girls from age 1 to 18 and the ages at which they were collected.
elements.sent-2411-140: This data set was provided by \cite{bib16 , the research project focused on the physical development of a cohort of individuals from infancy to adulthood.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{7_growth_sim.eps} \caption{Height of boys and girls} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{7_growth_epi_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_E$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{7_growth_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_H$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Curves and EE-plots of the Berkeley Growth Study data set} \label{fig:growth}
elements.sent-2411-141: The curves for boys and girls are depicted in Figure \ref{fig:growth (a), the $EE_E$-plot and $EE_H$-plot are shown in Figures \ref{fig:growth (b) and \ref{fig:growth (c), respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: We can see how the distributions for boys and girls are similar to each other, except for the oldest years where the girls overpass the height of the boys.
elements.sent-2411-143: Inside the the $EE_E$-plot and $EE_H$-plot we can also appreciate the similarity in the curves according the separation of both groups.
elements.sent-2411-144: The cross-validation results for this data set was done using 20-Folds and the results are given in Figure \ref{fig:results_growth .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth,keepaspectratio=true]{7_results.eps} \caption{Berkeley Growth Study accuracy metrics given by each classifier using 20-Fold cross-validation as train control} \label{fig:results_growth}
elements.sent-2411-145: The distribution patterns for boys and girls show similarity, with the exception of the eldest years, where girls surpass boys in height.
elements.sent-2411-146: In both the $EE_E$-plot and $EE_H$-plot, the curves demonstrate similarity for the youngest years.
elements.sent-2411-147: However, in the oldest years, a noticeable separation of datapoints becomes evident, underscoring the separation between the two groups.
elements.sent-2411-148: The cross-validation for this dataset utilized a 20-fold approach, and the results are depicted in Figure \ref{fig:results_growth .}
elements.sent-2411-149: To establish a benchmark, both the EE-classifier and the DD-classifier were implemented in the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-150: The results reveal variations in accuracy metrics among the classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-151: Despite fluctuations, an overall accuracy of approximately $\sim75\%$ demonstrates the classifiers efficacy in separating between boys and girls based on height.
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, the LDA classifier paired with the $EE_E$-plot shows the least favorable results, while the QDA-classifier along with the $EE_H$-plot emerges as the best performing combination.
elements.sent-2411-153: In comparison with the $DD^G$-classifier, the EE-classifier demonstrates narrower confidence intervals and a smaller gap between the 1st and 3rd quartiles, indicating greater robustness.
elements.sent-2411-154: This suggests that the EE-classifier provides a more stable and reliable performance in this context.
elements.figure-2411-008: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{8_tecator_sim.eps} \caption{Meat curves} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{8_tecator_epi_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_E$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{8_tecator_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_H$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Curves and EE-plots of the Tecator data set} \label{fig:tecator}
elements.sent-2411-155: The second dataset, referred to as Tecator, comprises spectrometric curves representing absorbance measured at 100 different wavelengths for pieces of meat, with fat percentages categorized into two groups: small ($< 20\%$) and large ($> 20\%$) \cite{bib17 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: The division of these curves into the respective fat percentage groups is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:tecator (a).}
elements.sent-2411-157: Notably, absorbance levels tend to be higher for pieces with larger fat percentages.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, in the EE-plots (Figures \ref{fig:tecator (b) and \ref{fig:tecator (c)), it becomes evident that the distributions of absorbance values for the two fat percentage groups are intertwined.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: This close proximity of distributions suggests potential challenges for classifiers in separating them between the two classes.
elements.sent-2411-160: The results presented in Figure \ref{fig:results_tecator outline the accuracy metrics obtained from a cross-validation process employing 50-Folds.}
elements.sent-2411-161: As intuitively anticipated, the classification rates are generally lower, reflecting the difficulty in separating the curves due to their close proximity.
elements.sent-2411-162: The accuracy metrics hover around $\sim70\%$, implying the capability to identify low-fat or high-fat curves at this classification rate.
elements.sent-2411-163: Despite the increased complexity of the dataset, the classifiers still demonstrate a reasonable level of effectiveness in distinguishing between the two categories.
elements.sent-2411-164: The most favorable outcomes are given when employing the QDA classifier along the $EE_H$-plot, despite a relatively higher confidence interval.
elements.sent-2411-165: Remarkably, the EE-classifier consistently proves competitive in terms of results, showcasing notable robustness in certain instances by yielding smaller range between 1st and 3rd quartile.
elements.sent-2411-166: This shows the effectiveness of the EE-classifier in handling the challenging distributions such as the Tecator dataset.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth,keepaspectratio=true]{8_results.eps} \caption{Tecator accuracy metrics given by each classifier using 50-Fold cross-validation as train control} \label{fig:results_tecator}
elements.sent-2411-167: The third and final dataset is the mithochondiral calcium overload (MCO) \cite{bib18 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: This dataset measures the MCO in isolated mouse cardiac cells every 10 seconds during an hour which can be raised by drugs.
elements.sent-2411-169: It consists of two groups, one which receives no drug (control) and another group that receives the drug (treatment) that can raise MCO levels.
elements.sent-2411-170: Both groups and their MCO can be seen on Figure \ref{fig:mco (a).}
elements.sent-2411-171: The EE-plots for this dataset are shown on Figures \ref{fig:mco (b) and \ref{fig:mco (c) where the curves show slight differentiation of the groups.}}
elements.figure-2411-010: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=10cm,keepaspectratio=true]{9_mco_sim.eps} \caption{Control \& treatment curves} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{9_mco_epi_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_E$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{9_mco_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_H$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Curves and EE-plots of the MCO data set} \label{fig:mco}
elements.sent-2411-172: The results for the MCO data set are shown in Figure \ref{fig:results_mco .}
elements.sent-2411-173: As we see in Figure \ref{fig:mco (a) the curves are intertwined, and in Figures \ref{fig:mco (b) and \ref{fig:mco (c) the EE-plots show small distinction between the groups, therefore the results as shown in \ref{fig:results_mco are not the best, with an average of correct classification of $\sim67\%$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: The best overall result was achieved by using the $DD^G$-classifier with the QDA method.
elements.sent-2411-175: However, the range between the 1st and 3rd quartiles is high, indicating a wider variability in performance.
elements.sent-2411-176: In contrast, the EE-classifier using the $EE_E$-plot with QDA also shows good classification results, along with lower confidence intervals, showing enhanced robustness in comparison.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth,keepaspectratio=true]{9_results.eps} \caption{MCO accuracy metrics given by each classifier using 30-Fold cross-validation as train control} \label{fig:results_mco}
elements.sent-2411-177: It is interesting to note that, despite the mean accuracy in some scenarios reflecting smaller classification rates, the cross-validation results often show a maximum accuracy of around $\sim100\%$ for most methods.
elements.sent-2411-178: This suggests that the EE-classifier is notably effective in classifying curves, even in scenarios where they have similar distributions.
elements.sent-2411-179: Furthermore, the consistent small ranges between the 1st and 3rd quartiles, as observed in both generated data and real datasets, underscore the robustness of the EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-180: This characteristic adds to its appeal as a reliable method capable of maintaining stable performance across various datasets, reinforcing its efficacy in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-181: The Standard and Poor's (S\&P) 500 stock value has been a huge focus between researchers, it aggregates the stock values for 500 different companies and provides an increase or decrease of the market for them combined in a single stock.
elements.sent-2411-182: The main challenge usually involves predicting a quantitative output value of the stock.
elements.sent-2411-183: Although, sometimes it is also desired to predict a categorical or qualitative output that shows whether the stock is increasing or decreasing \cite{bib19 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: In this type of classification problem, predicting a numerical value gets substituted by predicting whether a given stock market performance will fall into the Up or Down category.
elements.sent-2411-185: Therefore, the goal is to find a model that can predict the direction in which the market will move in a certain period of time.
elements.figure-2411-012: \captionsetup[subfigure]{justification=centering} \begin{subfigure}{.34\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=3cm]{10_case_sim.eps} \caption{S\&P 500 stock price curves} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{10_case_epi_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_E$-plot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth, height=4cm,keepaspectratio=true]{10_case_hipo_plot.eps} \caption{$EE_H$-plot} \end{subfigure} \caption{Curves and EE-plots of the S\&P 500 stock price data set} \label{fig:case}
elements.sent-2411-186: For this application, we received data for the stock values of 363 out of the 500 companies.
elements.sent-2411-187: We collected the closing stock values for each month from January 2005 to February 2023 for these companies.
elements.sent-2411-188: Subsequently, we created labels to indicate whether the S\&P 500 stock price went up or down compared to the previous month, corresponding to the stock values of these companies.
elements.sent-2411-189: The functional data array was then constructed with 218 observations, each containing 363 data points along with the corresponding labels.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth,keepaspectratio=true]{10_results.eps} \caption{S\&P accuracy metrics given by each classifier using 50-Fold cross-validation as train control} \label{fig:results_case}
elements.sent-2411-190: The experiment was based on the example given in Section 4.7 found on page 171 of \cite{bib19 where the classification problem consists of the categorical prediction of the market increase or decrease.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The data from January 2005 to December 2021 was used to predict if the market was going Up or Down for the dates between January 2022 to February 2023.
elements.sent-2411-192: Therefore, that data was separated as training and testing samples, on which we performed a 50-Fold cross-validation training and a prediction on the testing samples based on the trained models.
elements.sent-2411-193: The S\&P curves are depicted in Figure \ref{fig:case .}
elements.sent-2411-194: These curves (Figure \ref{fig:case (a)) exhibit considerable overlap, as evident in the EE-plot (Figure \ref{fig:case (b), (c)).}}
elements.sent-2411-195: This overlap, as discussed in prior sections and supported by experimental findings, poses a potential challenge for the classifier.
elements.sent-2411-196: The cross-validation results (Figure \ref{fig:results_case ) indicate that, on average, the models can predict whether the market will go Up or Down with an accuracy of approximately $\sim75\%$.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Notably, the EE-classifier's best performance is observed with LDA and QDA, although with a wide confidence interval for both the $EE_E$-plot and $EE_H$-plot.
elements.sent-2411-198: In this context, the EE-classifier demonstrates higher mean accuracy metrics than the $DD^G$-classifier, while showing smaller ranges between the 1st and 3rd quartiles.
elements.sent-2411-199: Regarding the prediction outcomes from the test sample, the accuracy metrics are detailed in Table \ref{tab:6_results .}
elements.sent-2411-200: Most of the metrics hover around $\sim50\%$, with the RF model using the $EE_E$-plot delivering the best result.
elements.sent-2411-201: These outcomes align with expectations, given the overlapping of datapoints evident in the EE-plots.
elements.sent-2411-202: However, it's worth noting that the DD-classifier also encounters challenges in classifying this data, emphasizing the complexity of the problem at hand.
elements.sent-2411-203: Nonetheless, exploring the results of the classifiers, particularly the RF model using $EE_E$-plot, we see that out of 14 months on which the market increases the models correctly predicts 10 of them, and we anticipate a definite market increase for the years 2021-2023.
elements.sent-2411-204: Despite the challenging nature of the dataset, a closer examination of the classifier results, especially the RF model using the $EE_E$-plot, one could say that, out of the 14 months in which the market shows an increase, the model correctly predicts 10 of them.
elements.sent-2411-205: This encouraging accuracy instills confidence, and we anticipate a market increase for the years 2022-2023 based on these predictive outcomes.
elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Testing prediction results for the S\&P 500 data}\label{tab:6_results} \tabcolsep=10pt \begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|} {Method} {MEI} {MHI} DD LDA 0.64 0.43 0.64 QDA 0.50 0.43 0.43 kNN 0.35 0.40 0.57 SVM 0.45 0.47 - RF 0.71 0.69 - \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-206: Even though the 2022-2023 data was not used to fit the training model, the level of accuracy of $\sim60\%$ is a good result for the S\&P 500 stock data, which can usually be hard to predict.
elements.sent-2411-207: This shows that the models act according to the relation between the companies stock value and fluctuation of the market.
elements.sent-2411-208: It is important also to note that the data set is quite small, and usually classification methods need larger data sets to be able to train and test appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-209: With larger history of the data, we could predict S\&P 500 stock fluctuation more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-210: In this article, we immersed into supervised classification methods for functional data.
elements.sent-2411-211: Specifically, we focused on the depth-based classification methodology and introduced the EE-classifier as an extremality-based classifier.
elements.sent-2411-212: The classifier was examined using modified epigraph and modified hypograph indexes, showing the distinct behavior of various groups within the $\mathbb{R ^d$ space of the EE-plots.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Several experiments were carried out to demonstrate the effectiveness of the classifier using both synthetic and real data, alongside a benchmark provided by the $DD^G$-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-214: The classifier showed great results across various experiments, comparing different curves with distinct distribution characteristics, including variations in centerline, amplitude, and dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-215: Even in scenarios where curves are closely intertwined, making data classification slightly more challenging, the method exhibited good accuracy results.
elements.sent-2411-216: Regarding classification methods, one might infer that the QDA classifier delivered superior outcomes in both training (cross-validation control) and testing.
elements.sent-2411-217: LDA proves to be among the top classifiers when dealing with curves from non overlapping distributions.
elements.sent-2411-218: As for the indexes, both MEI and MHI offer comparable accuracy metrics.
elements.sent-2411-219: However, in certain cases, the EE-classifier using MHI might shows better performance, making it an interesting question for further research and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-220: The benchmark set by the $DD^G$-classifier serves as a reference point, providing valuable insights into the performance of the EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-221: Throughout the entirety of the article, the EE-classifier consistently showcased competitive results, suggesting its potential as a robust and effective method for classifying diverse types of distributions.
elements.sent-2411-222: This pattern underscores the EE-classifier's reliability and strength across various scenarios explored in this research.
elements.sent-2411-223: Regarding the application to S\&P 500 stock values, the EE-classifier demonstrated a categorical prediction accuracy of approximately $\sim60\%$ for the fluctuations observed from January 2022 to February 2023.
elements.sent-2411-224: This outcome signifies the classifier's ability to correctly anticipate stock value increases during that period, aligning with the actual market trends between those months.
elements.sent-2411-225: For both the real datasets and the application, while the obtained results were promising, it would be intriguing to conduct experiments with larger datasets.
elements.sent-2411-226: A more extensive dataset could offer additional information for training methods, potentially enhancing their performance.
elements.sent-2411-227: In an initial analysis of the application, only five years were considered.
elements.sent-2411-228: As we expanded the dataset to ten years, the metrics showed an upward trend, and concurrently, the confidence intervals for the mean accuracy in cross-validation diminished.
elements.sent-2411-229: This trend suggests the potential benefits of utilizing more extensive datasets for improved model training and more reliable performance assessments.
elements.sent-2411-230: The classification method based on the extremality of functional data, gave a good performance and was able to classify the groups of every data set provided in each experiment and application in a positive light.
elements.sent-2411-231: Hopefully, this research leads to higher improvement of the method and new application can be found base on the EE-classifier.
elements.sent-2411-232: The authors would like to thank the Finance Laboratory at Universidad EAFIT for providing the data for the S\&P 500 stock value.
elements.sent-2411-233: This research was founded by the project \emph {``Design of new statistical technics for the classification of high dimensional data" with number 1119-11190072021 from Universidad EAFIT, Medellín, Colombia.}
